standard_description:
Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
32 students shared 6 pizzas equally. How much pizza will each student get?
a.   Find the exact answer.
b.   Check your work.
Problem 2
32 gallons of water is used to completely fill 6 fish tanks. If each tank holds the same amount of water, how many gallons will each tank hold?
a.   Find the exact answer. Write it as a fraction and as a mixed number.
b.   Determine which two whole numbers the answer is in between.
c.   Explain how the fractional part of the mixed number is related to the remainder in the problem.
d.   Check your work.
Problem 3
Solve the following problems. Think about the main differences between each one.
a.   A teacher has 21 batteries. Each calculator uses 4 batteries. How many calculators can the teacher fill with batteries?
b.   Four children can ride in a car. How many cars are needed to take 21 children to the museum?
c.   Ms. Cole bakes 21 cookies and wants to share them out fairly to 4 friends. How many cookies will each friend get?

items.question_content:
You are planning a movie marathon.  Each movie lasts $\dfrac32$ hours.  You want to watch $4$ movies nonstop. 

**How many hours will your movie marathon last?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]]  hours
hints:
Each movie lasts $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ hours.


[[☃ image 1]]

You want to watch $\blueD{4}$ movies nonstop.


[[☃ image 1]]

You can multiply $\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ to figure out how long your movie marathon will last.

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD3} {\maroonD2}\\\\
&={\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$
Your movie marathon will last $6$ hours.

Aliana uses beads that are $\dfrac54$ centimeters long to make a necklace. She uses $3$ beads all together.

**How many centimeters of beads will Aliana use for the necklace?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] centimeters
hints:
Each bead has a length of $\maroonD{\dfrac54}$ centimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

items.question_content:
Last week, Becky went to dance class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ hours.

This week, Becky went to dance class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ hour.

**Which week did Becky spend more total time in dance class?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Last week

Last week, Becky went to class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class last week: 

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\blueD 3} {\blueD2}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=\blueD{6}
\end{align}$

Becky went to class for $\blueD{6}$ hours last week.

###This week

This week, Becky went to class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{5 \times 1}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class this week: 

$\maroonD5\times\maroonD1=\maroonD5$

Becky went to class for $\maroonD{5}$ hours this week.

Last week |  | This week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{6}$ hours | $>$ | $\maroonD{5}$ hours
Becky spent more total time in dance class last week.

choices.content: She spent the same amount of time in dance class each week.

items.question_content:
Raul bought $6$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac23$ of them.

Omar bought $8$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac14$ of them.

**Who ate more tangerines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Eating a fraction of the tangerines is the same as eating that fraction times the number of tangerines each person had.

###Raul

Raul ate $\blueD{\dfrac23 \text{ of }6}$ tangerines.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{\dfrac23\times6}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Raul ate: 

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac23\text{ of }6} &=~ \blueD{\dfrac23 \times 6}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{2\times6}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{12}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD4
\end{align}$

Raul ate $\blueD4$ tangerines.

###Omar 

Omar ate $\maroonD{\dfrac14 \text{ of }8}$ tangerines.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{\dfrac14\times8}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Omar ate:

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\dfrac14\text{ of }8} ~&=~ \maroonD{\dfrac14 \times 8}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{1\times8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD2
\end{align}$

Omar ate $\maroonD2$ tangerines.

Raul |  | Omar
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD4$ tangerines | $>$ | $\maroonD2$ tangerines
Raul ate more tangerines.

choices.content: They ate the same number of tangerines.

items.question_content:
The pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits on it as the tomato plant has. The tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it. 

**How many fruits does the pepper plant have on it?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fruits
hints:
A tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it.

![9 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25f5c8fbc09be9480b3c740ba974dae49fdfe9be)

The pepper plant has $\dfrac23$ as many fruits as the tomato plant.

Let's break the $9$ tomatoes into $3$ equal groups.

![The same 9 circles in 3 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3c6172f2db0c563ee435bfa4cab1f0b0cfb48b21)

The pepper plant has $2$ of the $3$ equal groups.

![6 circles in 2 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a7b84b16f38612f53c8a4b0a69b9d739ac0e58c5)

There are $6$ peppers.

Since the pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits, we can also multiply $9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}$ to find how many fruits are on each plant.

$\begin{align}
&9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{9 \times 2} {3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{18}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac61\\\\
=&{6}
\end{align}$
The pepper plant has $6$ fruits on it.

khan_exercises.description: Practice writing a fraction as a mixed number and vice versa.
topic_path: Write mixed numbers and improper fractions

items.question_content:
**Write $\dfrac{5}{2}$ as a mixed number.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{2}}$ is the same as the fraction $\dfrac{1}{2}$ added $5$ times.

So we can write $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{2}}$ like this:

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{2}} = \underset{\greenD1}{\underbrace{\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}}} +  \underset{\greenD1}{\underbrace{\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}}}  + \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{2}} = \greenD{1}+\greenD{1} + \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ 

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{2}} = \greenD{2} + \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$
The mixed number is $2\dfrac{1}{2}$.

khan_exercises.title: Compare fractions and mixed numbers
khan_exercises.description: Practice comparing fractions and mixed numbers that have unlike denominators.
topic_path: Module 5: Fraction equivalence, ordering, and operations
Topic E: Extending fraction equivalence to fractions greater than 1

items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$2\dfrac{2}{5}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{34}{10}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{34}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{34}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10} + \dfrac{4}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{4}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ and $\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{2} <\green{3}$

Because $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} <\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} < \green{\dfrac{34}{10}}$

items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{7}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{7}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{3}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+\dfrac{3}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{1\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}}$ and $\green{1\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\blue{1} =\green{1}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}}$ and $\green{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $12$.

$\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}} = \blue{\dfrac{1}{12}}$  

$\dfrac{\green{3}\times3}{\green{4}\times 3} =  \green{\dfrac{9}{12}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{\dfrac{9}{12}}$
$\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{1\dfrac{9}{12}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{\dfrac{7}{4}}$

Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\green{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ and $\blue{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $8$:

$\dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{8}} = \dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{8}} $

$\dfrac{\blue{2}\times 2}{\blue{4}\times 2} = \blue{\dfrac{4}{8}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\green{\dfrac{1}{8}}  < \blue{\dfrac{4}{8}}$
$\green{3\dfrac{1}{8}} < \blue{3\dfrac{4}{8}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\green{\dfrac{25}{8}} < \blue{3\dfrac{2}{4}}$

items.question_content:
$4$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $4$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] square units
hints:
There are $4$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac45}$ of each square is shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}$ to find the total shaded area.
$\begin{align}
&\quad~~4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4 \times \blueE4}{\blueE5}\\\\
&=\dfrac{16}{5}
\end{align}$

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{16}{5}$ square units.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac15$ square units.

hints: We see a set of $10$ triangles. The set is split into $5$ equal sections, or *fifths*.

$\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$.

To find $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $1$ fifth. How many triangles are shaded?

We shaded $2$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $2$ fifths.


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $4$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $3$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $6$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $4$ fifths.

\dfrac{2}{5} \times 10 &= 4\\\\
\dfrac{3}{5} \times 10 &= 6\\\\
\dfrac{4}{5} \times 10 &= 8
\end{align}$
